seo.title: Recruitment/HR Data Extraction AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for data extraction AI agents in recruitment/hr. Covers EEOC guidelines, NYC Local Law 144, EU AI Act (high-risk).

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for resume and candidate data extraction in recruitment carries significant legal and ethical risk, as errors or biased outputs can trigger EEOC enforcement actions, NYC Local Law 144 penalties, and discriminatory hiring lawsuits. Structured extraction pipelines must be rigorously validated to ensure schema accuracy, field completeness, and bias-free processing across all protected characteristics. This checklist provides concrete, actionable controls to keep your AI hiring tools compliant, auditable, and defensible.

sections.heading: Bias Prevention & Fair Hiring Compliance
sections.description: Ensure the data extraction pipeline does not surface, infer, or propagate protected characteristics that could introduce discriminatory signals into downstream hiring decisions.

items.label: Exclude protected characteristic fields from extraction schema
items.description: Explicitly remove or block schema fields for age, race, gender, national origin, religion, disability status, and pregnancy from the structured output definition. Validate that LLM outputs never populate these fields even when source documents contain them.

items.label: Audit prompt templates for proxy discrimination
items.description: Review all schema-guided prompts for language that could cause the model to infer protected characteristics via proxies such as graduation year (age), sorority/fraternity membership (religion/gender), or neighborhood (race). Remove or neutralize such instructions before deployment.

items.label: Conduct pre-deployment disparate impact analysis
items.description: Run the extraction pipeline on a representative test dataset of at least 500 resumes with known demographic labels. Calculate selection rates across protected groups using the 4/5ths rule to detect adverse impact before going live.

items.label: Schedule annual bias audits per NYC Local Law 144
items.description: Engage an independent third-party auditor to conduct a bias audit of the AI screening tool annually. Publish the audit summary on your company website at least 10 business days before the tool is used on NYC candidates.

items.label: Validate extracted skills and experience fields for demographic neutrality
items.description: Test whether the model extracts equivalent skills and experience data from resumes with stereotypically different demographic names (e.g., Lakisha vs. Emily). Flag and remediate any statistically significant extraction gaps.

items.label: Implement a human-in-the-loop review gate for edge cases
items.description: Define confidence thresholds below which extracted candidate records are flagged for manual HR review rather than passed directly to ranking or filtering systems. Document the threshold logic and review SLA.

items.label: Log all extraction decisions with immutable audit trail
items.description: Store every LLM input, output, schema version, and timestamp in an append-only audit log. Retain logs for a minimum of 3 years to support EEOC investigation requests and litigation holds.

sections.heading: Candidate Notification & Transparency Requirements
sections.description: Meet mandatory disclosure obligations under NYC Local Law 144, the EU AI Act, and emerging state hiring bias laws by ensuring candidates are informed about AI use at every stage of the process.

items.label: Display AI use disclosure before application submission
items.description: Add a clear, plain-language notice on the job application page stating that AI tools are used to extract and process resume data. The notice must appear before the candidate submits any documents, not buried in terms of service.

items.label: Provide NYC-compliant candidate notification at least 10 days prior
items.description: For roles based in New York City, notify candidates in writing at least 10 business days before the AI screening tool is applied to their application. Include the tool's purpose, the data it processes, and how outputs influence hiring decisions.

items.label: Offer an accommodation request pathway for ADA compliance
items.description: Include a clearly labeled link or contact method in all AI disclosure notices allowing candidates to request an alternative, non-AI review process as a reasonable accommodation under the ADA. Document all accommodation requests and outcomes.

items.label: Publish the bias audit summary URL in candidate communications
items.description: Include a direct link to the publicly posted bias audit summary in all candidate-facing AI disclosure notices. Ensure the URL remains active and updated after each annual audit cycle.

items.label: Create a candidate data access and correction mechanism
items.description: Build a process allowing candidates to request a copy of their extracted structured data record, identify errors, and submit corrections before their application is evaluated. Define a response SLA of no more than 5 business days.

items.label: Document the legal basis for data processing under GDPR/CCPA
items.description: For EU and California candidates, document the specific legal basis (e.g., legitimate interest, consent) for processing resume data through an AI extraction pipeline. Include this in your privacy notice and data processing agreements with LLM vendors.

items.label: Maintain a candidate-facing FAQ on AI use in hiring
items.description: Publish a dedicated FAQ page explaining what data the AI extracts, how it is used in hiring decisions, who reviews the outputs, and how candidates can contest AI-influenced decisions. Update the FAQ within 30 days of any material change to the system.

sections.heading: Schema Validation & Extraction Accuracy
sections.description: Enforce strict schema compliance, field-level accuracy, and completeness validation to ensure the structured output reliably represents candidate qualifications without hallucination or data loss.

items.label: Define and version-control the canonical extraction schema
items.description: Maintain a versioned JSON Schema or Pydantic model defining all required, optional, and forbidden fields. Store schema versions in source control and tie each extraction run to a specific schema version in the audit log.

items.label: Enforce strict type validation on all extracted fields
items.description: Validate that every extracted field matches its declared type (e.g., date ranges as ISO 8601 strings, years of experience as integers, email as RFC 5322 format) before the record is accepted. Reject and flag records with type mismatches for human review.

items.label: Implement completeness scoring with minimum threshold gates
items.description: Calculate a completeness score for each extracted record based on the ratio of populated required fields to total required fields. Reject records scoring below 80% completeness and route them to manual extraction or re-processing.

items.label: Run ground-truth accuracy benchmarks on a labeled holdout set
items.description: Maintain a labeled holdout dataset of 200+ resumes with manually verified extraction outputs. Run the pipeline against this set monthly and require field-level F1 scores above 0.92 for critical fields (job titles, dates, skills) before any schema or prompt change is promoted to production.

items.label: Detect and reject hallucinated field values
items.description: Implement post-extraction validation rules that cross-check extracted values against source document text using exact or fuzzy string matching. Flag any extracted value with no corresponding text span in the source document as a potential hallucination.

items.label: Handle multi-format document ingestion with format-specific preprocessing
items.description: Build preprocessing pipelines for PDF, DOCX, HTML, and plain text resume formats that normalize encoding, remove headers/footers, and preserve section structure before LLM extraction. Test each format type independently in QA.

items.label: Validate date range logic and chronological consistency
items.description: Apply business rules to verify that employment end dates are after start dates, that education graduation years are plausible given stated degree levels, and that there are no overlapping full-time roles unless explicitly marked as concurrent. Flag violations for review.

items.label: Test extraction pipeline on adversarial and non-standard resume formats
items.description: Include resumes with creative layouts, graphics-heavy PDFs, non-English sections, and unconventional section headings in your QA test suite. Measure extraction degradation and set format-specific confidence thresholds accordingly.

sections.heading: Governance, Monitoring & Incident Response
sections.description: Establish ongoing operational controls to detect model drift, schema failures, and compliance violations in production, and define clear escalation paths for incidents involving discriminatory outputs.

items.label: Assign a designated AI system owner accountable for compliance
items.description: Formally designate a named individual (e.g., HR Technology Lead or Chief People Officer) as the accountable owner of the AI extraction system. Document their responsibilities for bias audits, candidate notifications, and regulatory reporting in a RACI matrix.

items.label: Define an incident response plan for discriminatory extraction outputs
items.description: Create a written incident response playbook specifically for AI hiring bias incidents. Include steps for immediate system suspension, candidate notification, EEOC self-reporting evaluation, root cause analysis, and remediation timelines. Test the playbook annually via tabletop exercise.

items.label: Monitor production extraction quality metrics on a weekly cadence
items.description: Track completeness scores, type validation failure rates, hallucination flags, and schema rejection rates in a real-time dashboard. Set alerting thresholds that trigger human review when any metric degrades more than 10% week-over-week.

items.label: Conduct quarterly disparate impact monitoring on live extraction outputs
items.description: Analyze production extraction records quarterly to detect emerging disparate impact patterns across protected groups. Compare pass-through rates, completeness scores, and field extraction accuracy by inferred demographic proxies. Escalate findings above 4/5ths rule thresholds immediately.

items.label: Maintain a model and vendor change management process
items.description: Require a full re-validation cycle (bias audit, accuracy benchmarks, schema compliance tests) before deploying any change to the underlying LLM, prompt templates, or extraction schema. Document approvals and test results in a change log.

items.label: Establish data retention and deletion schedules for extracted candidate records
items.description: Define retention periods for extracted candidate data aligned with EEOC record-keeping requirements (minimum 1 year for applicant records, 2 years for federal contractors). Implement automated deletion workflows and document exceptions for active litigation holds.

items.label: Conduct annual staff training on AI hiring tool compliance
items.description: Require all HR staff, recruiters, and hiring managers who interact with AI extraction outputs to complete annual training covering EEOC guidelines, NYC Local Law 144 obligations, ADA accommodation requirements, and how to identify and escalate potential bias incidents.

items.label: Register the AI system in your EU AI Act high-risk system inventory
items.description: If operating in the EU, register the recruitment AI extraction tool in your internal high-risk AI system register as required by the EU AI Act. Document conformity assessment steps, technical documentation, and human oversight measures before EU deployment.

notes.note: Any automated employment decision tool (AEDT) used to screen candidates for jobs based in New York City must undergo an independent bias audit within one year prior to use. The audit summary must be publicly posted on the employer's website, and candidates must receive written notice at least 10 business days before the tool is used on their application. Violations carry fines of up to $1,500 per violation per day.

AI-driven resume extraction tools that produce outputs used in hiring decisions are subject to EEOC Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures. If the tool produces adverse impact against any protected class (race, color, sex, national origin, religion), the employer bears the burden of demonstrating job-relatedness and business necessity. EEOC has explicitly stated that employers cannot outsource liability to AI vendors.

AI systems used for recruitment, CV sorting, and candidate evaluation are explicitly classified as high-risk under Annex III of the EU AI Act. Deployers must implement a risk management system, conduct conformity assessments, maintain technical documentation, ensure human oversight, and register the system in the EU database before deployment. Non-compliance penalties can reach €30 million or 6% of global annual turnover.

The Americans with Disabilities Act requires that AI hiring tools do not screen out qualified individuals with disabilities unless the criteria are job-related and consistent with business necessity. Several states including Illinois (AEDT Act), Maryland, and Washington have enacted or are advancing state-level AI hiring bias laws with independent audit and disclosure requirements that may exceed federal minimums. Compliance programs must track and incorporate state-specific obligations by hiring location.

cta.heading: Automate Your Recruitment/HR Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI recruitment extraction pipeline for bias, schema drift, and regulatory compliance gaps — so you stay audit-ready and defensible before the EEOC comes knocking.
